Title: The Spodoptera frugiperda L-aminoacylase degrades fatty acid-amino acid conjugates and promotes larvae growth on Zea mays

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: laboratory colony originally collected from non-Bt corn fields in Ruili, Yunnan, China
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in gut and salivary glands of fourth-instar larvae; preferentially up-regulated in salivary glands when feeding on maize leaves versus artificial diet; induced by cotton leaves but more strongly by maize leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in gut and salivary glands of fourth-instar larvae; preferentially up-regulated in salivary glands when feeding on maize leaves versus artificial diet; induced by cotton leaves but more strongly by maize leaves
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Hydrolytic turnover of herbivore-associated fatty acid-amino acid conjugates in oral secretions
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Fatty acid-amino acid conjugates (FACs), including volicitin, N-hydroxylinoleoyl-L-glutamine, and N-linoleoyl-L-glutamine
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: L-glutamine and corresponding fatty acid moieties
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes the ester/amide linkage of FACs in oral secretions, releasing L-glutamine and fatty acid moieties
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: FACs act as classic plant defense elicitors that activate plant MAPK signaling, jasmonic acid signaling, and herbivore-induced volatile emission
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduces FAC contents in oral secretions and promotes larval growth on maize leaves; loss of function increases FAC accumulation and strengthens maize defense responses
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SfruACY was identified from salivary gland transcriptomics as a maize diet-induced gene in Spodoptera frugiperda larvae. 2. Recombinant SfruACY degraded the major FACs in vitro and increased L-glutamine levels. 3. CRISPR knockout larvae accumulated more FACs in oral secretions and showed reduced growth on maize but not on artificial diet. 4. Maize attacked by SfruACY knockout larvae or treated with their oral secretions displayed stronger defense-related gene expression.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Salivary gland RNA-seq; qRT-PCR; recombinant protein enzymatic assay; LC-MS targeted metabolite profiling of FACs; CRISPR-Cas9 knockout; insect performance bioassay; maize transcriptomics
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Diet type (maize leaves, cotton leaves, artificial diet); feeding on maize seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq of salivary glands and maize leaves; SRA BioProject PRJNA1070368
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The gene is a conserved single-copy gene across examined Lepidoptera. Only one edited loss-of-function allele was obtained, so allele-specific effects cannot be fully excluded. Its ecological effect may also depend on diet nutrient composition and gut microbiota status.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for RNAi-based pest management or other precision control strategies aimed at impairing host adaptation of fall armyworm on maize, potentially with lower selection pressure than targeting essential genes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Knockout reduced larval performance on maize but not on artificial diet; broader ecological effects in natural populations with intact microbiota remain uncertain.
